coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence probe based on boron-doped carbon quantum dots for high selectivity “on-off-on” mercury ion sensing and cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Alan
Zhang, Yue
Wang, Xianghu
Xu, Qinhai
Li, Zhenjiang
Sheng, Liying
Yan, Lijun
coredata.dc:description: Mercury ions (Hg2+) are recognized as the typical inorganic contamination that can cause severe damage to the environment and humans. In this paper, boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs) with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 10.4% and good photoluminescent properties were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid and histidine as the carbon source and borax as the provider of boron. The obtained B-CQDs exhibited a bright blue emission under excitation wavelength of 365 nm. As a fluorescent probe, it appears excellent selectivity and sensitivity to assay Hg2+. When Hg2+ were added to B-CQDs, its bright blue fluorescence was obviously quenched. Under optimum conditions, the sensor shows a wide linear range (0 ~ 150 μM) and a low detection limit (2.5 μM). Interestingly, the quenched fluorescence of B-CQDs containing Hg2+ could be turned on by adding reducing agent EDTA-2Na or I-. Furthermore, it was applied successfully to assay Hg2+ in real sample. More importantly, B-CQDs was found to be nontoxic and can be used as bioimaging agent for MCF-7 cell labeling. The possible fluorescence quenching mechanisms of the probe are also discussed in detail. This study provides a new approach for fabricating a multifunctional fluorescent probe and a prospective application in environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boron-doped carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence probe
Hg2+ detection
Fluorescence recovery
Bioimaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: MTT assay and application of cell imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work reported here was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 52072196 , 52002199 , 52002200 , 52102106 , Major Basic Research Program of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2020ZD09 , the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2019BEM042 , ZR2020QE063 , the Innovation and Technology Program of Shandong Province under Grant No. 2020KJA004 , and the Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province under No. ts201511034 . We express our grateful thanks to them for their financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of B-CQDs, (b) particle size distribution histogram of B-CQDs, (c) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) of B-CQDs, elemental mapping images of B-CQDs: (d) C, (e) N, (f) B, (g) O.
(a)XRD patterns of B-CQDs, (b) FT-IR spectrum of B-CQDs, (c) XPS spectrum of B-CQDs, (d) High-resolution XPS spectrum of B 1s (e), C 1s, (f) N 1s, (g) Na 1s, (h) O 1s.

(a) Emission spectra of B-CQDs excited from 340 to 420 nm, (b) Excitation spectra of B-CQDs at different emission wavelengths (400–460 nm), (c) The maximum fluorescence intensity obtained by continuously measuring the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs, (d) the maximum fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs with different dilutions, (e) the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs, (f) the effect of storage time on the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of B-CQDs with different concentrations of Hg2+, λex = 370 nm. (b) The relationship between F0/F and the Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0 ~ 750 μM, F0 and F are the maximum fluorescence intensities of B-CQDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+. (c) The linear relationship between F0/F and the Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0 ~ 150 μM. (d)The fluorescence recovery spectra of B-CQDs containing Hg2+, λex = 370 nm.

(a) Normalized fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs in the presence of water (blank sample) and metal ions solutions (1 mM), respectively, λex = 370 nm. (b) The normalized fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs in the presence of water (blank sample) and anionic solutions (1 mM), respectively, λex = 370 nm. (c) When water (blank sample) and metal ion solutions (1 mM) were added into Hg2+ (1 mM) and B-CQDs (1/5) mixed solutions, respectively, their normalized fluorescence intensity.
a) UV-Vis absorption spectra of B-CQDs and B-CQDs containing Hg2+. (b) The fluorescence lifetime of B-CQDs and B-CQDs containing Hg2+.
The bright field image (a) and their corresponding laser confocal microscopic image (b) of MCF-7 cells cultured with B-CQDs for 16 h at 37 ℃.
Schematic illustration for the preparation B-CQDs and their application in Hg2+ detection and cell imaging.
Analytical performance of the present method with the other reported methods for the detection of Hg2+.
Analysis of Hg2+ in tap water using B-CQDs as a fluorescent probe (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fluorescence probe based on boron-doped carbon quantum dots for high selectivity “on-off-on” mercury ion sensing and cell imaging

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury ions (Hg2+) are recognized as the typical inorganic contamination that can cause severe damage to the environment and humans. In this paper, boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs) with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 10.4% and good photoluminescent properties were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid and histidine as the carbon source and borax as the provider of boron. The obtained B-CQDs exhibited a bright blue emission under excitation wavelength of 365 nm. As a fluorescent probe, it appears excellent selectivity and sensitivity to assay Hg2+. When Hg2+ were added to B-CQDs, its bright blue fluorescence was obviously quenched. Under optimum conditions, the sensor shows a wide linear range (0 ~ 150 μM) and a low detection limit (2.5 μM). Interestingly, the quenched fluorescence of B-CQDs containing Hg2+ could be turned on by adding reducing agent EDTA-2Na or I-. Furthermore, it was applied successfully to assay Hg2+ in real sample. More importantly, B-CQDs was found to be nontoxic and can be used as bioimaging agent for MCF-7 cell labeling. The possible fluorescence quenching mechanisms of the probe are also discussed in detail. This study provides a new approach for fabricating a multifunctional fluorescent probe and a prospective application in environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ion pollution in the environment has attracted more and more attention because it poses a serious threat to human health. The mercury ion (Hg2+) is listed as one of the most toxic and dangerous heavy metal ions, because it is easy to bioaccumulate and has strong interaction with sulfhydryl group in protein, which can cause severe damage to the environment and human body [1–4]. Even in small doses, Hg2+ may lead to irreversible damage to kidney, endocrine system, digestive system and central nervous systems owning to its serious toxicity [5]. In view of this situation, it is urgent to construct a feasible and effective strategy for selective determination Hg2+ efficiently, quickly and sensitively in the environment, especially in drinking water and in living organisms.

Up to now, the common methods for assaying Hg2+ are atomic absorption [6], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [7,8], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICPAES) [9], electrochemical method [10], and fluorescence spectroscopy [11], etc. Among these methods, fluorescence spectroscopy is becoming more attractive due to the advantages of high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. And so far, several fluorescent chemosensors containing typical fluorophore moieties as signaling unit have been reported [12–14]. However, traditional fluorescent probes often have disadvantages such as high toxicity and complicated synthesis process. In short, it is necessary to develop new fluorescent materials with superior performance and greener synthesis methods for Hg2+ assay.

Carbon dots, which are also known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are a class of newly emerging fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with ultralow particle size. CQDs have gained tremendous attention due to their exceptional properties including high fluorescence intensity, photostability against photobleaching, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, water solubility and adjustable emission wavelength [15–18]. Based on these important properties, CQDs can be served as promising candidates in various applications such as photocatalysis, drug delivery, bioimaging, and especially analytical sensing [19–23]. However, the generally poor stability and low quantum yield (QY) of CQDs have limited their applications. The relevant reports on CQDs manifested that CQDs with high QY, more stable optical properties as well as excellent catalytic properties have been synthesized via doped heteroatoms such as N, P, S and B [24–27]. Therefore, the synthesis of heteroatomic doped quantum dots is a very meaningful work, which can be used in heavy metal ions determination and biological imaging.

Inspired by the previous works, B-CQDs with high quantum yield was investigated by a green, simple, one-step hydrothermal treatment, using citric acid and histidine as the carbon source and borax as the provider of boron. B-CQDs emitted blue fluorescence at 360 nm and it showed selective and sensitive fluorescence quenching response towards Hg2+ (turn off). In presence of EDTA-2Na or I-, the quenched fluorescence of B-CQDs/Hg2+ system was recovered (turn on, Scheme 1). What's more, B-CQDs could also be applied to determinate Hg2+ in tap water sample, which showed that B-CQDs had practical application value. The cell viability study using the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)− 2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay of the MCF-7 cell indicated that the as-synthesized B-CQDs are nontoxic. It can be used as bioimaging agent for MCF-7 cell imaging. The work not only acquired B-CQDs by one-pot hydrothermal method for determining Hg2+ and MCF-7 cell imaging, but also supplied a reference for fabricating other hetero-doped CQDs with high-performance.

Citric acid (C3H8O7·H2O, ≥99.5%), borax (Na2B4O7·10 H2O, ≥99.5%), histidine (C6H9N3O3, ≥99%), concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, ≥98%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥96.0%), iron nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)·9 H2O, ≥ 98.5%), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6 H2O, ≥ 99.0%), calcium nitrate tetrahydrate (Ca(NO3)2·4 H2O, ≥ 99.0%), chromic nitrate (Cr(NO3)3·9 H2O, ≥ 99.0%), cadmium chloride hemipentahydrate (CdCl2·1/2 H2O, ≥99.0%), silver nitrate (AgNO3, ≥99.8%), nickel sulfate hexahydrate (NiSO4·6 H2O, ≥98.5%), copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5 H2O, ≥99.0%), potassium chloride (KCl, ≥99.5%), manganese chloride (MnCl2·4 H2O, ≥99.0%), zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4·7 H2O, ≥99.3%), sodium sulfate anhydrous (Na2SO4, ≥97.0%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, (Shanghai, China). Aluminum nitrate (Al(NO3)3·9 H2O, ≥ 99.0%) was purchased from Tianjin BASF Chemical Co., Ltd., (Tianjin, China). Barium chloride (BaCl2·2 H2O, ≥99.5%), EDTA (C10H16N2O8, ≥99.5%) and potassium iodide (KI, ≥99.0%) were obtained from Tianjin Guangcheng Chemical Co., Ltd., (Tianjin, China). Magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, ≥99.0%) was supplied by Tianjin Ruijinte Chemical Co., Ltd., (Tianjin, China). Lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2, ≥ 99.0%) was purchased from Shanghai Jinshan Chemical Co., Ltd., (Shanghai, China). Mercury nitrate (Hg(NO3)·1/2 H2O, ≥ 97.0%) was purchased from Jiangyan Huanqiu Co., Ltd., (Jiangsu, China). 3-(4,5-Dimethyl-2–2thiazolyl)− 2,5-diphenyl-2 H-tetrazoliumbromide (MTT) was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was obtained from Tianjin Fuyu Chemical Co., Ltd., (Tianjin, China). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) was purchased from Wuhan Procell Life Technology Co., Ltd., (Wuhan, China). Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) was acquired from Guangzhou saiguo biotech Co., Ltd., (Guangzhou, China). The above chemical reagents were analytical grade, without further purification. All the aqueous solutions were prepared by using double distilled water.

The morphology and particle size distributions of B-CQDs were conducted via Hitachi (Tokyo, Japan) JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) instrument. The elemental mapping images were obtained by using a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM-6), which equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) equipment. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were surveyed using Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were carried out using an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS D8, Germany, Cu Kα radiation). Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was conducted with Nicolet is10 (Thermo Scientic, CaF wafer technique, USA). Fluorescence spectra were measured by fluorescence Spectrophotometers (HITACHI F-4600, Japan). The photographs of B-CQDs solutions under UV light (365 nm) irradiation were displayed by ultraviolet analyzer (Shanghai Kang Huasheng instrument manufacturing Co., Ltd.). The fluorescence lifetime B-CQDs was gauged by Edinburgh FLS980 all functional steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). UV-Vis spectrum was recorded on Lambda 35 UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, Singapore). The fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells were taken on laser scanning confocal microscope (Olympus Fluoview FV 1000, USA). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were conducted using a Malvern laser particle size meter equipped with a 4.0 mW laser.

B-CQDs were prepared through one-step hydrothermal method. 0.4 g citric acid, 0.4 g borax and 0.5 g histidine were dissolved into 30 mL deionized water and magnetically stirred 5 min. Then the solutions were transferred into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined reaction autoclaves (50 mL) and heated at 180 ℃ for 12 h. After cooling the reaction autoclaves to room temperature naturally, the bright yellow solutions were centrifuged at 8000 rpm min−1, the clear solutions were filtered with 0.22 µm micro filtration membrane and dialyzed for one day in dialysis bags (Molecular weight = 1000 Da). Subsequently, B-CQDs were stored for further characterization and use.

The quantum yield (QY) of B-CQDs was calculated according to the following equation:
(1)
Φ
C D s
=
Φ
R
(
I
C D s
/ I
R
)
(
A
R
/
A
C D s
)
(
η
C D s
/
η
R
)
2
Where ‘Ф’ is the quantum yield, ‘I′ stands for the fluorescent intensity, ‘A′ represents the absorbance and ‘η’ refers to the refractive index of the solvent used (1.33 for both solvents, η CDs/η R = 1). The subscript ‘CDs’ and ‘R′ symbolize B-CQDs and reference, respectively. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (literature quantum yield 0.54 at excitation wavelength of 360 nm) was taken as a reference [28,29].

All B-CQDs solutions were used after diluting 5 times with deionized water, which was noted as B-CQDs (1/5). 0.9 mL different concentrations Hg2+ (10−3 M, 2 × 10−4 M, 1.7 × 10−4 M, 1.3 × 10−4 M, 10−4 M, 6.7 × 10−5 M, 3.3 × 10−5 M, 2 × 10−5 M, 10−5 M, 2 × 10−6 M, 10−6 M) were added into 0.3 mL B-CQDs (1/5), respectively. After incubating at room temperature for 2 min, the sensitivity of B-CQDs for Hg2+ determination was evaluated. In the selectivity experiment, the metal ions (Fe3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Cu2+, K+, Al3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Na+) and the anions solutions (AC-, NO3 -, Cl-, SO4 2-, SO3 2-) were added into 0.3 mL B-CQDs (1/5), respectively. For the interference experiment, B-CQDs (1/5) containing Hg2+ (1 mM) were mixed with other metal ions (Fe3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Cu2+, K+, Al3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Na+), respectively. The concentration of the ions was all 1 mM. For the fluorescence recovery experiment, EDTA-2Na (1 mM) and KI (1 mM) was added into B-CQDs (1/5) containing Hg2+ (1 mM), respectively. The fluorescence intensities of mixed solutions were measured at 370 nm, and the slits were 5 nm whether in excitation or emission. All solutions were prepared in deionized water at room temperature.

To assess the feasibility of B-CQDs as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Hg2+ in practical application, the tap water samples collected from our lab (Qingdao, China) were selected as the real samples for the experiment. Firstly, the samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min and then were filtered through 0.22 µm membrane to remove impurities. Subsequently, the standard Hg2+ solutions with the concentration of 10, 50 and 100 μM was injected to the real samples, respectively. Finally, Hg2+ in the real samples was determined according to the standard addition recovery method and each sample was determined 3 times in parallel. The recovery rate was calculated as the following equation:
Recoveryrate ( % ) =
FoundConcentration
AddedConcentration
× 100 %

To assessed the toxicity of B-CQDs, MTT assay was carried out by using MCF-7 cells. MCF-7 cells were purchased from Shanghai Saibai Kang Biology. Firstly, MCF-7 cells were seeded into 96-well plates and incubated in incubator (5% CO2, 37 °C, 24 h). Whereafter, B-CQDs with different concentrations were added into each well and incubated for another 24 h. After that, 100 μL 0.5 mg mL−1 MTT solution was added into each well and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. Then, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 100 μL) added to each well containing the aforementioned substance, and the mixture were placed on a shaker shock for 10 min (protected from light). The cells without B-CQDs but with 20 μL 0.01 M (pH = 7.4) phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were taken as control. Finally, the absorbance of the solution was acquired at 490 nm via a microplate reader. The cell viability was calculated on the basis of the formula in the following:
c e l l
v i a b i l i t y ( % ) =
(
O
D
t r e a t e d
O
D
c o n t r o l
)
× 100 %
Where OD
treated is the absorbance with B-CQDs, OD
control is the absorbance without B-CQDs (control).

To investigate the bioimaging potential of B-CQDs, cell imaging experiments were designed and conducted using MCF-7 cells. MCF-7 cancer cells (4 ×104/cell) were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and 1% Penicillin-Streptomycin (Pen Strep) at 37.5 ℃ in a 5% CO2 environment, and the cells were incubated with B-CQDs in the cell culture dishes for 16 h. Media in the dishes were discarded and the cells were gently rinsed twice with 0.01 M PBS (pH= 7.4). The fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells were taken on a laser scanning confocal microscope with an excitation wavelength of 405 nm. For comparison, the cells incubated with DMEM but without B-CQDs were also carried out at the same condition.

The morphology of B-CQDs was investigated by TEM. As depicted in Fig. 1a, the synthesized B-CQDs were approximately sphere-like shape, uniform in distribution with no clear agglomeration, and had a well mono-dispersed. As Fig. 1b shows, based on TEM image, the particle size distribution of B-CQDs was relatively narrow, ranging from 3.00 to 5.50 nm with an estimated average diameter of 4.20 nm. To further evaluate the particle size distribution of B-CQDs, DLS analysis was performed and presented in Fig. 1c. As displayed in Fig. 1c, the size distribution profile manifests that the average diameter of B-CQDs in aqueous solution about 5.12 nm, which is in agreement with the results of TEM. To characterize the space distribution of different elements in B-CQDs, the mapping was conducted (Fig. 1d-g). As shown in Fig. 1d-g, C, N, B and O present in B-CQDs and they are evenly distributed throughout B-CQDs.

XRD analysis was performed to characterize the phase and composition of B-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 2a. Apparently, a broad diffraction peak centered at around 26.5° could be attributed to (002) plane, which is consistent with JCPDS card no. 65–6212, indicating B-CQDs was graphitic-like carbon-based materials. The information about functional groups on the surface of B-CQDs was investigated through FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2b). The peak at 1089 cm−1, 1160 cm−1, 1397 cm−1, 1586 cm−1 and 1629 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibrations of C-O, C-C, C-N, CN and CO band, respectively. The band at 2876 cm−1 and 3042 cm−1 are attributed to C-H stretching vibration. The peaks at 3149 cm−1 and 3358 cm−1 are designated as the stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H band, respectively. The FT-IR results manifested that B-CQDs contains abundant functional groups on the surface of B-CQDs. In addition, the element composition was further demonstrated via XPS spectrum and the results were shown in Fig. 2(c-h). The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 2c revealed five peaks at 191.8, 284.7, 531.9, 400.4 and 1071.4 eV, which were ascribed to B1s (2.02%), C1s (59.25%), N1s (7.58%), O1s (29.94%) and Na1s (1.21%), respectively. The high-resolution B1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) existed two major peaks at 190.4 and 191.9 eV, which were attributed to BC2O and BCO2, respectively, indicating B was doped into CQDs. Fig. 2e depicted the high-resolution C1s spectrum.

The peaks centered at 284.7, 285.7 and 288.1 eV associated with C-H/C-C, C-N/CN and O-CO, respectively. Two peaks located at 399.8 and 400.4 eV in high resolution spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 2f) were owing to CN and C-N/N-H, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) could be deconvoluted into two peaks around about 531.0 and 531.9 eV, ascribed to C-O and CO/-OH, respectively. In the high-resolution Na1s spectrum (Fig. 2h), the signal of Na at 1071.4 eV was observed. XPS results provided the evidence that the as-prepared B-CQDs containing many functional groups, which was consistent with FT-IR results. The functional groups not only improved the water solubility and facilitated the surface modification of B-CQDs, but also interacted with metal ions.

Fig. 3a displayed the fluorescence emission spectrum of B-CQDs under the different excitation wavelengths from 340 to 420 nm. As shown in Fig. 3a, the emission wavelengths were gradually red-shifted as the excitation wavelength increased. It may be caused by the auxochrome such as –OH, which contains unshared electrons, resulting in the conjugate action of p-π and the formation of multi-electron conjugate system, making the spectrum redshift. The strongest fluorescence intensity was acquired when the excitation wavelength was 370 nm. Therefore, the excitation wavelength was set as 370 nm in the subsequent experiments. Besides, as the emission wavelengths varied from 400 nm to 460 nm, the excitation wavelength also appears red shift (Fig. 3b). The fluorescence emission of excitation dependent is attributed to the size effect and surface defects [30]. When the emission wavelength is 450 nm, B-CQDs has the maximum fluorescence intensity. The photostability of B-CQDs was also investigated.

As shown in Fig. 3c, the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs was almost unchanged after continuously measured for 40 times, indicating B-CQDs possess excellent photostability. Fig. 3d depicted the effect of B-CQDs concentration (c, 1/2c, 1/3c, 1/4c, 1/5c, 1/6c, 1/7c, 1/8c, 1/9c, 1/10c, 1/20c, 1/50c, 1/100c) on fluorescence intensity. With B-CQDs concentration diluted from c to 1/100c, the fluorescence intensity increased first and then decreased, when B-CQDs concentration was diluted to 1/5c, the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs was strongest, which is caused by the concentration effect. When the concentration of B-CQDs was higher, the fluorescent nanoparticles are easy to agglomerate due to the influence of van der Waals force. So, the size of the nanoparticles increased, resulting in a decrease in quantum efficiency, and then the fluorescence intensity is reduced. For these reasons, as the concentration of B-CQDs was diluted to 1/5c, the agglomeration of fluorescent nanoparticles decreased, the size decreased, the dispersion became more uniform, the quantum effect increased, and the fluorescence intensity increased. When the dilution multiple was less than 1/5c, the total amount of B-CQDs decreased, leading to the decrease of fluorescence intensity. The quantum yield (QY) of B-CQDs was calculated to be 10.4% using quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY: 0.53 at 365 nm) as the standard.

The influence of pH on the fluorescence stability of the as-obtained B-CQDs was also researched. As shown in Fig. 3e, the fluorescent intensity of the as-obtained B-CQDs only changed slightly with pH value increasing from pH 1.75–11.59. Furthermore, the storage stability of the as-obtained was evaluated. As given in Fig. 3f, the fluorescent intensity only changed by 5% after storage 14 days. The aforementioned results indicated that the as-obtained B-CQDs possessed outstanding fluorescent stability in various conditions.

Under the optimal conditions, the sensitivity of the as-prepared B-CQDs sensor was evaluated by adding different concentrations of Hg2+ solution into B-CQDs aqueous solution. As illustrated in Fig. 4a, upon increasing Hg2+ with the concentration from 0 to 750 μM, the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs at around 440 nm is gradually quenched. It can be seen from Fig. 4b,c that the fluorescent quenching efficiency F 0/F has a good linear relationship with Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0 ~ 150 μM, which fits to the Stern-Volmer equation: F 0/F= 1 +K SV[C], where F 0 and F represent the fluorescent intensity of B-CQDs at 450 nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively, K SV is the Stern-Volmer’s constant, [C] is the concentration of Hg2+. The linear regression equation was expressed as F 0/F= 1 + 0.02c(μM) (R=0.9950) and the KSV value is 2 × 104 M−1 [31]. Additionally, the detection limit was calculated to be 2.5 μM based on three times signal-to-noise ratio, which is superior to some previous reports ( Table 1).

The fluorescence recovery properties of B-CQDs after adding Hg2+ (1 mM) was estimated by using EDTA-2Na (1 mM) or I- (2 mM). The control experiment and fluorescence recovery experiments were performed according to Table S1 and the results was depicted in Fig. 4d. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs significantly quenched after adding Hg2+. Interestingly, when EDTA-2Na (Na2H2Y) or I− is added into the aforementioned mixed solutions, the “off” state of B-CQDs quenched by Hg2+ can be turned “on” and the quenched fluorescence intensity is recovered to almost the same as the original fluorescence intensity. The possible fluorescence recovery mechanism was as follows: when EDTA-2Na was added into B-CQDs containing Hg2+, Na2H2Y reacts with Hg2+ to yield HgH2Y on the basis of Eq. (1). when I- was added into B-CQDs containing Hg2+, I- can react with Hg2+ to generated HgI2 according to Eq. (2). The interaction between Hg2+ and EDTA-2Na or I- is stronger than that between Hg2+ and B-CQDs, the fluorescence is generally recovered.
(1)
Hg
2 +
+ Na
2
H
2
Y =
HgH
2
Y + 2Na
+
(2)
Hg
2 +
+ 2I
-
=
HgI
2
The results indicated that Hg2+ can significantly quench the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs, and I- or EDTA-2Na can recover the fluorescence of B-CQDs containing Hg2+.

To estimate the selectivity of this sensing system for Hg2+ detection, the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs in the presence of various different metal ions (Hg2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Cu2+, K+, Al3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Na+) and anions (SO4 2-, Cl-, NO3 -, SO3 2-, Ac-, S2O5 2-) were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5(a, b), the normalized fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs obviously decreased with the addition of Hg2+, whereas, there is almost no change after adding the above metal ions or anions, demonstrating B-CQDs possess charming selectivity towards Hg2+. Additionally, the anti-interference of the as-prepared B-CQDs probe was also investigated. Water (blank sample) and metal ion solutions (1 mM) were added into B-CQDs (1/5) containing Hg2+ (1 mM), respectively. The fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was determined and the results were revealed in Fig. 5c. Evidently, compared to the blank sample, the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs was significantly quenched when Hg2+ and metal ions coexist. Besides, the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared B-CQDs has no significantly inhibited or enhanced when Hg2+ is mixed with the interference metal ions, indicating the as-prepared B-CQDs possess excellent anti-interference. The aforementioned results indicated that the as-prepared B-CQDs probe exhibit the charming selectivity and anti-interference for Hg2+ determination.

The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of B-CQDs and B-CQDs containing Hg2+ were examined. As illustrated in Fig. 6a, there was a characteristic absorption peak centered at 300 nm, attributing to n-π * electronic transition in B-CQDs. After adding Hg2+, the intensity of absorption peak at 300 nm dramatically weakens. The possible reason is that the presence of Hg2+ affected the surface state of B-CQDs, which led to fluorescence quenching. In addition, to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the spectrum characteristics of B-CQDs+Hg2+ and B-CQDs were conducted. As shown in Fig. S1, an obvious overlap could be observed between the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of B-CQDs+Hg2+ and the excitation spectrum of B-CQDs, which attributed to an internal filtration effect (IFE) [37]. To further investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of B-CQDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+ was assessed via a multidimensional time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) method. As shown in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence lifetime of B-CQDs is 5.81 ns. After addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence lifetime of B-CQDs+Hg2+ (τ1/τ2) was 1.43 (32.48%)/5.77 (67.52%) ns, and the total fluorescence lifetime was 4.36 ns. The fluorescence lifetime is shortened obviously in the presence of Hg2+, demonstrating the dynamic fluorescence quenching mechanism.

To further explore the practical application ability of B-CQDs, it was served as a fluorescent probe to assay Hg2+ in real tap water samples. The recovery experiments were carried out through standard addition recovery method. The standard Hg2+ solutions with the concentration of 10, 50 and 100 μM in the real samples were spiked into B-CQDs, respectively. The detection concentrations of Hg2+ were calculated according to the linear equation. Meanwhile, for comparison with our method, Hg2+ in real tap water samples was determined parallelly via inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). The results were shown in Table 2. As illustrated in Table 2, the sample recovery rates in tap water changed from 99.7% to 101.0% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is less than 3.1%, which were in accordance with the results of ICP-MS analysis, manifesting B-CQDs as a fluorescent probe has potential applicability for assaying Hg2+ in real tap water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTT assay and application of cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the application of B-CQDs as fluorescent probes for cell imaging, the cytotoxicity of B-CQDs against cancer cells was conducted through standard MTT assay by using MCF-7 cells as the model. As displayed in Fig. S2, MCF-7 cells still presented viability higher than 90% after incubation with B-CQDs in the range of 0–100 μg mL−1, manifesting B-CQDs possessed very low toxicity to cells, which would be potentially applied for cell imaging or other biomedical applications. Due to the satisfying low cytotoxicity, cell imaging based on B-CQDs were performed to further prove their feasibility in biological applications and the results were displayed in Fig. 7. Fig. 7a was the bright field image of MCF-7 cells cultured with B-CQDs for 16 h at 37 ℃. It can be observed from Fig. 7a that MCF-7 cells were evenly distributed. Fig. 7b represented the dark field image and the MCF-7 cells incubated with B-CQDs revealed quite bright blue fluorescent under 405 nm excitation wavelengths. For comparation, the bright field image and their corresponding laser confocal microscopic image of MCF-7 cells cultured with DMEM for 16 h at 37 ℃ was also investigated (Fig. S3). Remarkably, after cultured with DMEM, no fluorescent was observed under 405 nm excitation wavelengths. The aforementioned results indicated that the strong fluorescence attributed to B-CQDs. It demonstrated B-CQDs could easily penetrate the cell membrane and translocate into cells by endocytosis, and can be labeled MCF-7 cells.

In summary, B-CQDs were successfully fabricated using citric acid, histidine and borax as raw material through a simple one-step hydrothermal route. B-CQDs displayed fascinating optical propertied including good QY of 10.4%, high stability at different pH values and ionic strength, photostability, storage stability. Due to the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs were selectively quenched by Hg2+, B-CQDs was used as a fluorescence probe for assaying Hg2+. The IFE and the dynamic fluorescence quenching collectively affected the fluorescence quenching of B-CQDs by Hg2+. It displayed a wide linear range from 0 to 150 μM with a detection limit of 2.5 μM. I- or EDTA-2Na can effectively recover the fluorescence of B-CQDs containing Hg2+. Furthermore, it can be detected Hg2+ in real samples with satisfactory results. More significantly, B-CQDs with excellent nontoxicity and biocompatibility was also successfully used as MCF-7 cell marker. The present work has manifested that the as-obtained B-CQDs reveal potential not only as a fluorescence probe for assaying Hg2+ in real samples but also a clinical application in the near future.

Alan Meng: Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing – Original draft preparation. Yue Zhang: Data curation, Formal analysis. Xianghu Wang: Investigation, Validation. Qinhai Xu: Methodology, Investigation. Zhenjiang Li: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision. Liying Sheng: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Lijun Yan: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work reported here was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 52072196, 52002199, 52002200, 52102106, Major Basic Research Program of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2020ZD09, the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2019BEM042, ZR2020QE063, the Innovation and Technology Program of Shandong Province under Grant No. 2020KJA004, and the Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province under No. ts201511034. We express our grateful thanks to them for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129150.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric determination of Cr(VI) based on a dual-emission fluorescent nanoprobe using carbon quantum dots and a smartphone app
Microchim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.X. Yao, Q.R. Liu, N. Zhao, J.M. Liu, G.Z. Fang, S. Wang, Ratiometric determination of Cr(VI) based on a dual-emission fluorescent nanoprobe using carbon quantum dots and a smartphone app, Microchim Acta 188 (2021) 89, https://doi.org/10.1007/s00604–021-04747–8.
